204608.transcribe: I love your notion of, you know, intelligence perhaps requiring the capacity or intelligence perhaps having the capacity to enable efficient heuristics to navigate the solution space. We agree with you. Some can be more efficient, some can be less efficient, but efficiency seems like an important tenet of intelligence. So I like that view a lot. I also like your question in number four. Can we stop AI if it goes south? I like that you're not just assuming that we can or cannot stop it. You are investigating that question. You are wondering about that question. I love that. And yes, what you said in number five resonates with me a lot because human agency is absolutely required for judiciary warfare, at least judiciary warfare stuff. Healthcare and airspace tech, yes, I agree for the time being. Maybe I can get good enough that we don't need agency and can rely on AI completely, but I don't know. But yes, it's probably safe to just have the agency there. But for judiciary and warfare, absolutely, absolutely. I like how you say that unlike software engineering, those fields do not tolerate false positive. Absolutely love that.
204608.polish:
I love your notion that intelligence may require the capacity to enable efficient heuristics for navigating the solution space. Some heuristics can be more efficient than others, but it seems like an important tenet of intelligence. 

Your question in number four about whether we can stop AI if it goes awry is insightful. I commend you for not simply assuming that we can or cannot halt it; instead, you are actively investigating that question, which is commendable. 

In response to number five, I resonate with your point about the necessity of human agency in judicial warfare. While I think that in fields like healthcare and airspace technology, we might eventually rely on AI without human intervention, it seems prudent to maintain human agency for now. However, in judicial and warfare contexts, human agency is absolutely essential. I also appreciate your observation that, unlike software engineering, these fields cannot tolerate false positives. Your insights are valuable and well-articulated.

204902.transcribe: You make an interesting point about depth in what LLMs produce or the current AIs produce, because do we have a good understanding of depth in anything, whether it's their output or our output? That's something we should explore probably in class, because without that, we are unable to really measure or assess whether something that the AI is saying is deep or not or how that compares with human depth or the depth in human communication. Very good point about quantum computing, by the way, and we're going to explore that more when Professor Kate Smith comes to our class and talks about quantum computing. And very good question in number four, are we always going to be superior to AI? And will they ever be sentient?
204902.polish:
You raised an interesting point about the concept of depth in the outputs produced by large language models (LLMs) and current AI technologies. It prompts us to consider whether we truly understand depth in any context, including both AI outputs and our own. This is a topic worth exploring further in class, as it is essential for assessing the depth of AI-generated content compared to human communication.

I also appreciate your insightful comment regarding quantum computing. We will have the opportunity to delve deeper into this topic when Professor Kate Smith joins us to discuss it in class.

Additionally, your question about whether humans will always be superior to AI and the possibility of AI achieving sentience is thought-provoking. These are critical issues that warrant further discussion.

206187.transcribe: I absolutely love and appreciate the fact that you acknowledge that to answer the question, we need to first define rigorously what thinking means and then address the question. That said, in the case of this question, thinking basically is whatever we feel like or we think thinking might mean. But yes, of course, in the end, we have to define it, otherwise we cannot come to terms with it or agree with each other. So, yeah. Also great answer in number 3. Also a good question in number 4, whether machines can truly mimic human consciousness. In this case I don't know if it should be mimic or basically literally replicate because I can give you the illusion of consciousness perhaps in a machine but that would be mimicry, right? But I think what you are interested in is whether machines can actually replicate consciousness, like they can actually be conscious.
206187.polish:
I appreciate your recognition that we need to rigorously define what "thinking" means before addressing the question. In the context of this particular question, thinking can be somewhat subjective, as it may encompass whatever we feel or believe it to mean. Nonetheless, it is essential to establish a clear definition to facilitate understanding and agreement.

Your response to question number 3 is very good. Additionally, you posed a thought-provoking question in number 4 regarding whether machines can truly mimic human consciousness. I would suggest considering whether the term "mimic" is appropriate, or if "replicate" might be more accurate. A machine could potentially give the illusion of consciousness, which would be mimicry. However, it seems you are more interested in whether machines can genuinely replicate consciousness and possess actual awareness.

207343.transcribe: I like your definition of thinking, and I would tend to agree with you about how AI actually think. I'm curious to know why you put the word invented in quotes. I have my own, you know, rant about invention versus discovery. You might have heard about that. You might have heard that rant in CS248. If not, you might hear me talk about that again in this class, but I'm really curious to know why you put the word invented in quotes.
207343.polish: I appreciate your definition of thinking, and I agree with your perspective on how AI thinks. I'm curious about your use of quotes around the word "invented" :) I have my own thoughts on the distinction between invention and discovery, which I may have shared in CS348. If you haven't heard my views, I might discuss it again in this class :) However, I would like to understand *your* reasoning for using those quotes around the word.

212119.transcribe: So, the stochastic parrots argument has been brought up by others as well. It used to be much more commonly stated than it is nowadays. But it is still a notion that a lot of people put forth. That said, if you actually think about the design of an LLM, by design they cannot remember their inputs. So, they cannot quite have the memory that being a stochastic parrot would require. However, it's something definitely worth discussing. Also, it's interesting that your definition of thinking includes the requirement that unique observation has to come out. Something that did not exist in the world before. As far as whether AI models have gone beyond human control in labs, I don't know if that exact scenario where something wrong has happened in a lab situation has occurred. But definitely AI has gone beyond human expectations slash intent in certain circumstances, including in my own experience. There are instances, people and I have observed where AI has done something without having been asked to do it. And I unfortunately agree with your comparison of AI with the nuclear bomb in terms of the amount of harm that AI can bring. So, we are definitely working with lightning in a jar.
212119.polish:
The stochastic parrots argument has been raised by others and was more commonly discussed in the past, yet it remains a notion that many still present. When considering the design of a large language model (LLM), it's clear that these models, by design, cannot remember their inputs. This limitation means they lack the memory required to be considered stochastic parrots. Nonetheless, this topic is certainly worth discussing.

I find it intriguing that your definition of thinking includes the necessity for unique observations that did not previously exist in the world. Regarding whether AI models have exceeded human control in laboratory settings, I'm not aware that something wrong has happened in a lab setting per se, but Yes, AI has gone beyond user expectations in terms of not following intent. I have personally had such experiences as well, where AI has performed actions without being explicitly instructed to do so.

I also resonate with your comparison of AI to a nuclear bomb in terms of the potential harm it can cause. We are indeed working with something as unpredictable as "lightning in a jar."

213248.transcribe: I like your use of the word experience, the quote unquote experience, because yeah, I mean, I feel the same way that these machines can actually think and the way they connect concepts or their knowledge inside, like internally to produce a new concept that feels like, you know, it's that it's thinking because it's like all the knowledge it has is its experience and it's putting, it's drawing from the experience and thinking by producing new stuff.
213248.polish: I appreciate your use of the term "experience" in your response. I share your perspective that these machines can indeed think in a certain way. Their ability to connect concepts and draw from their internal knowledge to create new ideas resembles a form of thinking. It is as if they are using their accumulated knowledge as their experience to generate new outputs.

213686.transcribe:
very interesting points in #3. I particularly like your Ship of Theseus argument. I did wonder about the contradiction as well, and then I saw you mention it yourself :) I took the contradiction to mean that while you thought AI could conquer all forms of thinking, it's the current AIs that lack that capability due to not having the kind of experiential memory that getting burned by fire gives us. as for quantum, that's a whole other world of possibilities, so yes, who knows -- quantum computing might be the thing that brings real thinking to machines. we're scheduled to get a quantum computing talk, so perhaps we'll learn something along these lines. one other things i very much like in what you said is he possibility that you "think they'll also discover different aspects of thought that we haven't come up with yet because we're approaching it from a different angle." totally agree. it's just like Deep Blue's "strange" pawn move that baffled Kasparov in '97 or AlphaGo's similar move in 2015 or '16 that took Lee Sodol and whole world of Go by surprise, as neither move fell within what humans would ever think up.

absolutely love the first two sentences of #4, especially AI orienting itself in terms of us! a brilliant thought/question that you should bring up if apt during a class discussion. it's worth exploring as a group.

"Will it lead us out of the end of the world into a new world, or will it keep us in this awful place forever?" -- this is a gem! perhaps i think so because i myself have wondered this, so i'm biased that way.

overall, i wanna say that if i enjoyed reading all this, and if this was your first writing in this class, i can't wait to read the weekly thoughts!
213686.polish:
You made very interesting points in response #3. I particularly appreciated your Ship of Theseus argument. I noticed you addressed the contradiction yourself, which was insightful. I interpreted the contradiction to mean that while you believe AI could eventually conquer all forms of thinking, current AIs lack that capability because they do not possess the experiential memory that we gain from experiences, such as getting burned by fire.

Regarding quantum computing, it opens up a whole new world of possibilities. It’s exciting to think that quantum computing might be the key to enabling real thinking in machines. We are scheduled to have a talk on quantum computing, so I hope we can learn more about this topic.

I also resonated with your idea that we might discover different aspects of thought that we haven't yet considered because we are approaching it from a different angle. This reminds me of Deep Blue's unexpected pawn move that baffled Kasparov in 1997, or AlphaGo's surprising move in 2016 that took Lee Sedol and the entire Go community by surprise. Both moves were outside the realm of human thought.

I absolutely loved the first two sentences of response #4, especially the idea of AI orienting itself in terms of us. This is a brilliant thought and a question worth bringing up during class discussions.

Your statement, "Will it lead us out of the end of the world into a new world, or will it keep us in this awful place forever?" is a gem. I find it particularly compelling because I have pondered this question myself.

Overall, I want to express how much I enjoyed reading your responses. If this was your first writing in this class, I am eager to read your weekly thoughts!

214131.transcribe: I agree with you that most people's reservations with calling AIs or at least LLMs intelligent have to do with the whole next work prediction scheme or I don't know if it should be called a scheme but this process of next work prediction but I'm glad that you're open to the possibility that these machines still can be considered to think because we don't actually know what thinking means. I like your prediction about how we may not be able to differentiate between an artificial human and a real human in 10 years. As far as the 100 years goes, you're talking about incorporating smart and intelligent AIs into our society. For me, I'm not even sure what society means in 100 years really but yeah, that's an interesting thought. I love your question in number four that you're really interested to think about how AI is bounded by language or is it even bounded by language, right? And how it will challenge the notion of God for those who are religious. I think there will be some people who will keep believing in God the way they still do because that is independent of what happens in terms of AIs becoming all-knowing and what not. Regardless of the evolution of AI, I think for some people religion will remain intact but I can imagine for some people that definitely shifting.
214131.polish:
I agree that many people's reservations about calling AIs, particularly large language models, intelligent stem from their reliance on next-word prediction. I appreciate your openness to the idea that these machines might still be considered as thinking entities, especially since we do not fully understand what thinking entails.

I find your prediction about our inability to differentiate between an artificial human and a real human in ten years intriguing. Regarding the notion of society in a hundred years, I'm not even sure what society will mean at that time, but it's definitely an interesting question about the integration of intelligent AIs.

I also love your question in number four about whether AI is bounded by language. This inquiry challenges our understanding of concepts like God, particularly for those who are religious. I believe some individuals will continue to hold their beliefs regardless of advancements in AI, as their faith is independent of these developments. However, I can also envision that for some, the evolution of AI could lead to a shift in their religious perspectives.

237058.transcribe: I very much like your simple but elegant definition of thinking. It's not complicated, and it seems very, very objective, and I quite agree with how you're looking at thinking, that it's basically the act of processing facts and generating opinions or doing something accordingly, right? I mean, what more do we need? That pretty much sums up thinking, if you ask me. So I agree with you. Interesting point about thinking of themselves, which is something you mentioned as something that AI might never be able to do, the self-reflection, or I forget what the word is for that. I think it's called introspection. Introspection is something that they might not be able to do, but who knows, right?
237058.polish:
I appreciate your simple yet elegant definition of thinking. It is straightforward and appears very objective. I agree with your perspective that thinking involves processing facts and generating opinions or actions based on those facts. This definition effectively captures the essence of thinking.

You also raised an interesting point about self-reflection, which you suggested AI might never be able to achieve. I believe another word for self-reflection is introspection. While it seems that AI may struggle with this capability, it remains an open question.

238492.transcribe: Very good answer in the first paragraph of question two, because you recognize up front that thinking should be defined well, but of course in this, in the context of this particular question, we are, we are thinking more loosely about how we feel about thinking, but yes, you're absolutely correct. Thinking must be defined, but we don't even know exactly what thinking is. And I like how you're saying, I like what you said about why you think machines can think and the justification you have given for that. I think your second paragraph in question two is also profound. It's a very good, very systematic and detailed dissection of the problem of defining what thinking means. Oh, and I really like the question of the divergence that you want your magic wand to show you, the AI divergence, human and current AI divergence. That's what I was referring to. It's excellent. That's an excellent point. You ask really good questions. I really like the questions you've asked in number four.
238492.polish:
You gave a very good answer in the first paragraph of question two by recognizing the importance of defining thinking. In the context of this question, we are considering a more subjective perspective on our feelings about thinking. You are correct that thinking must be defined, even though we may not fully understand what it entails.

Your second paragraph in question two is also profound, offering a systematic and detailed analysis of the challenges in defining thinking. Additionally, I found your inquiry about the divergence between humans and current AI particularly insightful. This is an excellent point and demonstrates your ability to ask thought-provoking questions. I also commend you for the questions you posed in number four; they are very well articulated.

240023.transcribe: Interesting argument about cause and effect being a requirement for thinking. Hmm, I didn't think of that. Worth exploring in class.
240023.polish: I found your argument about cause and effect being a requirement for thinking to be interesting. I hadn't considered that perspective before. It is definitely worth exploring further in class.

241180.transcribe: Good thoughts that you shared here. A couple of things about number one is about the probabilistic guessing machine. I'm not so sure that I would call an LLM a probabilistic guessing machine. It does output a probability, but the machine itself is very deterministic. There is actually nothing probabilistic about the machine itself. So that's something to keep in mind, and we can probably discuss that further in class. As far as creativity goes, that's also something we should, you know, I encourage you to bring up in class whenever apt, maybe during the philosophy lecture, maybe in some other lecture, but if there's a good place to bring it up, we can explore creativity as well. I love how you're questioning yourself. You said, whatever I said above, part of me is skeptical about it. I like that mindset that even though you have opinions, you are open to those opinions being, you know, wrong or you're just open about other possibilities. That's fantastic. You're also asking the questions, what if AI is already thinking? What if they're already conscious? Good questions. And here's the more profound question, or not more profound, but it's a very profound question. How could this happen just based on images and words without any understanding of the real world slash world model? That's fantastic.
241180.polish:
You shared some insightful thoughts in your responses. Regarding your first point about the probabilistic guessing machine, I would like to clarify that I wouldn’t characterize a large language model (LLM) in that way. While it does output probabilities, the underlying mechanism is quite deterministic. This distinction is important, and I look forward to discussing it further in class.

On the topic of creativity, I encourage you to bring it up during our discussions, especially in the philosophy lecture or any other relevant context. It’s a valuable area for exploration.

I appreciate your self-reflection, particularly your expression of skepticism about your own views. It’s commendable that you maintain an open mindset, recognizing that your opinions could be challenged or revised. 

Your questions about AI, such as whether it is already thinking or conscious, are thought-provoking. Additionally, your inquiry into how such capabilities could arise from images and words without a real-world understanding is a profound one. I look forward to delving deeper into these topics with you.

241621.transcribe: Aha, you're bringing up consciousness as a requirement for thinking. That's interesting. There's no way to refute that with any proof. Of course, we don't know exactly what consciousness is and what its role is in intelligence or in thinking. But of course, there are counter arguments that can be given for thinking and consciousness. But yeah, definitely an interesting point. Yep. Simple innovation is definitely something we can expect from AI in 5, 10, and 100 years. I don't know if it's going to take a hundred years to conquer cancer. My hope is it'll take much less time than that. Let's hope in the next 5 to 10 years. Programmers, 5 to 10 years. I don't know. It seems like programming is going to go away. Programming is going to go away or should go away in the next two years. But yeah, maybe you're right because programmers will stick around because there's so much code already written, legacy code that needs to be maintained. Yeah, good question about what's stopping AI from developing self-consciousness. Thank you.
241621.polish:
You raised an interesting point about consciousness as a requirement for thinking. While we cannot definitively prove or refute this connection, it is clear that our understanding of consciousness and its role in intelligence and thinking is still evolving. There are certainly counterarguments regarding the relationship between thinking and consciousness, which adds depth to the discussion.

I agree that we can expect simple innovations from AI in the next 5, 10, and even 100 years. However, I am hopeful that advancements in areas like cancer research will occur much sooner than a century. Ideally, we should see significant progress within the next 5 to 10 years. At least that's my hope if not a prediction.

Regarding programming, I agree that programming will become less relevant in the near future. That said, programmers will continue to play a crucial role. given the substantial amount of legacy code that requires maintenance, which will keep programmers in demand for quite some time. For newcomers, though, learning program doesn't promise to carry much value.

Your question about what is preventing AI from developing self-consciousness is thought-provoking. Thank you for bringing it up.

265703.transcribe: It seems you have identified quite a few different aspects of LLMs that you are considering in a flawed or limited. One of them I'd like to address, which is what you said about LLMs being probabilistic. Well, even though they output probability, if you actually think about the design, they are deterministic. They work in a very precisely deterministic way. They're not quite probabilistic. So that's something to keep in mind when thinking about the strengths and weaknesses of LLMs. And then you also make a point about consciousness, which I know will come back up in class when we explore intelligence more and question whether consciousness is necessary for intelligence or vice versa. Good point about the experience of love, happiness, sadness being biological processes. The question is, could we mimic them or replicate them in machines in some way, without the biological processes, right? Yeah, I, too, agree that LLMs are not just a wave that will soon pass. This is definitely here to stay. And it's going to evolve in ways that we can't imagine. There are some we can. And I think for the most part, we can't imagine how this is going to play out. And it might not be limited to LLMs. AIs might take a different form altogether, not just LLMs. And then the evolution, the long-term evolution is really unpredictable. I don't think anybody knows where it's going to go.
265703.polish:
You have identified several aspects of large language models (LLMs) that you seem to consider flawed or limited. I would like to address one of them: your point about LLMs being probabilistic. While they do output probabilities, their design is fundamentally deterministic. They operate in a precisely deterministic manner, which is an important distinction to keep in mind when evaluating the strengths and weaknesses of LLMs.

You also raised an interesting point about consciousness, which I anticipate will come up again in class as we explore the relationship between intelligence and consciousness. Specifically, we will question whether consciousness is necessary for intelligence or vice versa. Your observation about the experiences of love, happiness, and sadness being biological processes is noteworthy. The critical question is whether we could mimic or replicate these experiences in machines without the underlying biological processes.

I agree with your assessment that LLMs are not merely a passing trend; they are here to stay and will evolve in ways we cannot fully predict. While we can envision some potential developments, the overall trajectory of this technology remains largely uncertain. It is also possible that artificial intelligence may take forms beyond just LLMs, and the long-term evolution of AI is unpredictable. No one can definitively say where it will lead.

266826.transcribe: i like that you're not dismissing the possibility that ai's think, perhaps differently than how humans do.
266826.polish: I appreciate that you are not dismissing the possibility that AI may think, albeit in a manner different from human thought processes.

267269.transcribe: Very nice and simple definition of thinking. I quite agree with you. Every one of us has that question, you know, brewing in our mind.
267269.polish: I appreciate your clear and straightforward definition of thinking. In your answer to question four, I completely agree with you; it’s a question that resonates with all of us.

272611.transcribe: That's a very good question you ask in number four. Does it matter if AI is actually conscious or not? Yeah, it's something we should explore in class. I've never thought about that. Maybe machines don't need to be conscious. But then we have to wonder why are we conscious? Do we need to be conscious? Why did we need to be conscious in the first place? Are we even conscious? Right? These are questions we should definitely explore if we have a chance in class.
272611.polish: You raised an excellent question in #4 regarding the significance of AI consciousness. This is a topic worth exploring in class, as it challenges our understanding of consciousness itself. I find it intriguing to consider whether machines need to be conscious at all. This leads us to deeper questions about our own consciousness: Why are we conscious? Do we need to be conscious? What was the purpose of our consciousness in the first place? Are we truly conscious? These are important questions that I would like us to explore further if time allows.

287745.transcribe: i don't know exactly what key points kris will address in his talk this time around, but he has in the past talked about intuition in this class, so if he does that, i encourage you to bring up your point about intuition, inner feeling, and evidence and factual statements. these three things are worth doing a deep dive on.
287745.polish: I don't know exactly what Kris will address in his upcoming talk. However, he has previously discussed intuition in this class. If he revisits that topic, I encourage you to share your insights about intuition, inner feelings, and evidence/factual statements. These three aspects are worth exploring in depth.

287757.transcribe:
Good dissection of what an AI coding agent needs to do to produce code per our demands. Are you likening this process to thinking then? Curious to know if that was your intent in #2.

"Accessing this universal consciousness that is built into our biology." -- Whoa! This one's making me think hard! We should talk about this, so I encourage you to bring this up in class if and when apt.
287757.polish:
You provided a strong analysis of what an AI coding agent needs to do to generate code according to our specifications. I am curious if you intended to liken this process to thinking in your response to question #2.

Your statement about "accessing this universal consciousness that is built into our biology" is quite thought-provoking. I encourage you to bring this up in class when the opportunity arises, as it could lead to an engaging discussion.

287797.transcribe: That's a good point you make about sentience. That's a hard nut to crack. Just like consciousness, sentience is also very difficult to define and for lack of a better word, quantify. How would we even achieve it? What does it even mean? We feel it and we can communicate it because we have that shared feeling, shared experience of sentience, but will machines ever achieve it? That's a very good question. Not sure if machines ever will or what that means. And good question about Pandora's box as well.
287797.polish: You raised an excellent point about sentience. It is indeed a challenging concept to define and quantify, much like consciousness. I appreciate your inquiry into how we might achieve a clear understanding of it. While we can feel and communicate our shared experiences of sentience, it remains uncertain whether machines will ever attain this quality. Your question about the implications of this possibility, akin to opening Pandora's box, is also thought-provoking.

287800.transcribe: Interesting point about experience. On the one hand, I agree that, you know, we, at least in our conscious brain, we rely on experience for our ability to think, but at the same time, where is that experience stored? If you think about it from a neuroscientific point of view, then it's the neurons that are encoding the experience in some form of memory. And the current AIs, at least LLMs, also have memory, not in a storage sense, but like the neurons encode memory. So, in some ways, you could say, or in a way, you could say that they also possess experience, and that's where their thinking comes from, because those experiences are, you know, getting activated to produce the final output. But, yes, it definitely begets or warrants discussion. A profound question about whether it's worth living if there are no challenges in life. Yep. Programming is coming to an end for humans. That's for sure. And your observations are a match mine about, you know, your observations of using AI to code. I'm doing entire projects now, and it's mind blowing how good the AI can be. And a very good point about Neuralink. God knows what's going to happen when that thing becomes mainstream.
287800.polish:
You raised an interesting point about experience. I agree that, at least in our conscious minds, we rely on experience for our ability to think. However, it prompts the question of where that experience is stored. From a neuroscientific perspective, neurons encode experiences in some form of memory. Current AI models, particularly large language models (LLMs), also have a form of memory—not in a traditional storage sense, but similar to how neurons encode memory. In this way, one could argue that these AIs possess a form of experience, as their outputs are generated by activating these encoded experiences.

It's a very good question about what the meaning of life might be if there are no more challenges left.

Yes, I believe we are approaching a point where programming may become less relevant for humans. Your observations about using AI for coding resonate with me; I am now able to complete entire projects with the assistance of AI, and it is remarkable how effective it can be.

Additionally, your point about Neuralink is well taken. The implications of that technology becoming mainstream are unpredictable and could have significant consequences.

287801.transcribe: "In these aspects I think AI systems are able to form connections between the knowledge they already have and apply them in a way that helps them solve problems." -- Brilliant! This seems simple enough as a concept, yet most people don't seem to entertain this possibility at all!
287801.polish: "The idea that AI systems can form connections between existing knowledge and apply it to solve problems is straightforward yet often overlooked by many." -- I find this insight to be brilliant.

287805.transcribe: Wow, that's an interesting example that you gave to explore whether a crocodile can think or not. You're thinking in terms of its response to a situation. Does it only act on stimuli or does it actually reason, right? As for the sense of self, I'm open to accepting that machines can do have a sense of self. But it's just that I don't think that's the case right now. In the future, I'd totally be open to it. But for now, something like SONNET 4.6 cannot introspect. So I would say it doesn't have a sense of self. And I like how you, you know, you give good examples of justification to arrive at the conclusion that you struggle to easily to so easily give AIs the mantle of thought. Instead of just claiming that they cannot think, you're actually working up working out a justification for that. And I like that approach. Whether they can or cannot think, whether I agree or not, you gave a good justification. That's a good explanation and that's what I like. As far as your question in number four about how AI will affect the workforce and how it might, you know, define the rest of your life, don't be terrified. Don't worry. We will have a whole class dedicated to this discussion when Professor Hatim Rahman comes and talks to us about AI in the workplace.
287805.polish:
You provided an interesting example to explore whether a crocodile can think. You framed the discussion around its responses to situations, questioning whether it acts solely on stimuli or if it can actually reason. I like that.

Regarding the sense of self, I am open to the idea that machines could possess a sense of self in the future. However, I do not believe that is the case at this moment. For instance, something like SONNET 4.6 cannot introspect, so I would argue it does not have a sense of self.

I appreciate how you presented strong justifications for your conclusion that it is challenging to attribute thought to AIs. Instead of simply claiming they cannot think, you worked through a rationale for your position. I value that approach, regardless of whether I agree with your conclusion. Your explanation was well-articulated and thoughtful.

In response to your question about how AI will affect the workforce and potentially shape your future, I encourage you not to be terrified. We will have an entire class dedicated to this topic when Professor Hatim Rahman joins us to discuss AI in the workplace.

288357.transcribe: It's interesting that you say that even though AI will be big in our art and psychology, that you don't think AI will fully understand those things. We can definitely explore this in class.
288357.polish: I find it interesting that you believe AI will have a significant impact on art and psychology, yet you don't think it will fully understand those concepts. I look forward to exploring this topic further in class.

290550.transcribe:
you haven't said what you'd define thinking as. curious to know that. care to drop me a line by email taking a stab at that definition?

interesting prediction in #3. never thought of it this way -- that larger models and more data might actually get in the way of achieving general intelligence.

love your point about the comparison of what's happening with large-scale ai model training vs. the pursuit of elegant mathematical and scientific solutions, the latter, in my opinion, being more likely to yield the true fundamentals of intelligence.
290550.polish:
I noticed that you haven't provided your definition of thinking. I would be curious to know your perspective on that. Please feel free to email me with your thoughts.

Your prediction in question #3 is intriguing. I had never considered that larger models and more data might hinder the achievement of general intelligence.

I also appreciate your comparison between large-scale AI model training and the pursuit of elegant mathematical and scientific solutions. I agree that the latter is more likely to reveal the true fundamentals of intelligence.

290812.transcribe: Good thoughts in number two, even though I would give the counterargument that number one, I don't see why unique ideas would be necessary for thinking, even though that is worth exploring. But secondly, as far as the Chinese room goes, I could argue that my brain, like the neurons don't actually know what they're doing, so they're behaving just like the Chinese room. The fact that I, the person, can't even understand what I'm doing is basically a result of or an output of those neurons themselves. So the neurons are computing this understanding that I have of what the neurons are doing. So maybe the systems can be designed to do the same thing or the systems can automatically become that way. A very interesting question in number four, where you say about the most pressing question about AI you have, I don't know if you've seen the movie Ex Machina, but they show that Ava is very curious and has the drive to learn on her own, so that's very interesting that you are asking something like that.
290812.polish:
You presented good thoughts in your response to question two. However, I would counter that unique ideas may not be necessary for thinking, although this is certainly worth exploring. 

Regarding the Chinese Room argument, I can argue that my brain functions similarly; the neurons do not actually understand what they are doing, which parallels the behavior of the Chinese Room. My own conscious understanding is, I'd argue, essentially an output of the neurons themselves. They compute my understanding of their actions, suggesting that systems could be designed to achieve a similar level of function or could naturally evolve to do so.

In question four, you raised an interesting point about the most pressing question regarding AI. If you have seen the movie Ex Machina, you might recall that Ava exhibits curiosity and a drive to learn independently. This aspect of AI is indeed fascinating and merits further discussion.

296416.transcribe: Interesting point about thinking, because you brought up, you know, probability distributions, so that's something, if we have a chance, I'd like to explore in class, how does a probability distribution relate to thinking? We can definitely speculate about that.
296416.polish: You made an interesting point about thinking, particularly in relation to probability distributions. I would like to explore this topic further in class. For example, how does a probability distribution relate to our thinking processes? I believe we can engage in some meaningful speculation on this subject.
